methodology_sections: 1. Conservation Laws - Solvency, inflation checks, supply integrity
2. Relationship & Ratio Invariants - Collateralization, rate consistency
3. Economic Monotonicity - Yield accumulation, exchange rate direction
4. Incentive & Game Theoretic Integrity - Attack cost vs profit, slashing
5. Fee & Yield Consistency - Fee accounting, yield distribution

invariants.description: The Bridge contract must not pay out more ETH for a message than the defined value and fee.
invariants.attack_scenario: If the bridge pays out more than the message's value and fee (e.g., via reentrancy or double-processing), it will become insolvent and unable to process other users' messages.
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_bridge_payout_limit(bytes32 msgHash) public view { assert(ethPaid[msgHash] <= messages[msgHash].value + messages[msgHash].fee); }

invariants.description: The Vault's balance of a canonical ERC20 token must be greater than or equal to the total supply of all its bridged counterparts across all destination chains.
invariants.attack_scenario: An attacker exploits a logic flaw to withdraw canonical tokens without burning the corresponding bridged tokens, leaving the bridged tokens unbacked and causing protocol insolvency.

invariants.description: The msg.value provided when sending a message must exactly equal the sum of the message value and the relayer fee.
invariants.attack_scenario: If a user can send a message where msg.value < value + fee, the bridge assumes a liability larger than the assets it received, leading to a slow drain of the bridge's ETH reserves.
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_message_funding(Message memory message, uint256 msgValue) public pure { assert(msgValue == message.value + message.fee); }

invariants.description: A message's status can only transition forward and can never revert from a terminal state (DONE, FAILED, RECALLED) to a pending state (NEW, RETRIABLE).
invariants.attack_scenario: If an attacker can reset a message's status from DONE to NEW, they can replay the message processing, double-spending funds on the destination chain.
invariants.invariant_logic: oldStatus == DONE || oldStatus == FAILED || oldStatus == RECALLED => newStatus == oldStatus
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_status_monotonicity(bytes32 msgHash, Status newStatus) public view { Status oldStatus = messageStatus[msgHash]; if (oldStatus == Status.DONE || oldStatus == Status.FAILED || oldStatus == Status.RECALLED) { assert(newStatus == oldStatus); } }

invariants.description: Bridged tokens can only be minted by the designated Vault or an authorized migrating address.
invariants.attack_scenario: If an unauthorized user can mint bridged tokens, they can inflate the supply infinitely and drain the canonical token vault upon bridging back.
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_mint_access() public view { assert(msg.sender == migratingAddress || msg.sender == resolve('erc20_vault', true)); }

invariants.description: A canonical ERC721 token must either be locked in the Vault, or its bridged counterpart must exist on the destination chain, but never both simultaneously owned by a user.
invariants.attack_scenario: An attacker duplicates an NFT by bridging it and then exploiting a vulnerability to withdraw the original canonical NFT without burning the bridged version.
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_nft_conservation(uint256 tokenId) public view { bool locked = IERC721(canonical).ownerOf(tokenId) == address(this); bool bridgedExists = BridgedERC721(bridged).exists(tokenId); assert(locked == bridgedExists); }
